mappings.rationale: Directly covers chemical reactions including acid-base, oxidation-reduction, reaction kinetics, catalysis, and thermodynamic aspects central to this section.
Directly covers reaction thermodynamics, chemical kinetics, equilibrium, activation energy, and electrochemistry discussed throughout this section.
Covers energy changes in chemical reactions, free energy, entropy, chemical equilibrium, and thermodynamic relations central to this section.
Covers organic reaction mechanisms including nucleophilic and electrophilic substitution, addition and elimination reactions discussed in this section.
Covers how molecular structure determines chemical reactivity, reaction mechanisms, and the factors influencing the course of reactions.
Covers the historical development of theories concerning chemical reactions and the growth of major concepts discussed in this section.
Relevant to understanding the basic reactivity of elements and the conservation of mass and energy in chemical transformations.
Directly relevant to catalysis, reaction rates, enzyme kinetics, and biological oxidation-reduction reactions discussed in this section.
Relevant to oxidation-reduction reactions in biological systems and acid-base equilibria in biochemical processes discussed in this section.
Relevant to photochemical reactions, the photochemical process, and the role of light in driving chemical reactions discussed in this section.
Directly relevant to the section's coverage of oxidation-reduction reactions in combustion and flames.
Relevant to understanding how periodic properties determine chemical reactivity and the classification of reactions.
Relevant to acid-base equilibria in aqueous solutions, solvents affecting reaction rates, and electrochemical reactions discussed in this section.
Relevant to the effects of isotopic substitution on chemical reaction rates and mechanisms discussed in this section.
Covers fundamental concepts of matter transformation and energy changes relevant to the basic principles of chemical reactions.
Relevant to the experimental measurement of reaction rates, equilibrium constants, and calorimetric methods discussed in this section.
Relevant to the development of theoretical frameworks for understanding chemical reactions, including the growth of major theories discussed in this section.
